lessons.title: Rotational Motion Basics
lessons.content:
**Hook.** A spinning bike wheel, a merry-go-round, a clock's hands, a fan blade — lots of things move by *turning* instead of going in a straight line. This kind of motion is called **rotation**: every part of the object circles around a fixed center. That center line is called the **axis** — the imaginable rod the object spins around.

**Build.** To describe spinning we don't ask "how far did it travel?" — we ask "how much did it turn?" The amount of turn is the **angle**, often written with the Greek letter $\\theta$ (theta). One full turn all the way around is $360$ degrees, which is the same as $2\\pi$ **radians**. A radian is just another way to measure angle, sized so that one full circle is $2\\pi$ (about $6.28$) radians — handy because it links nicely to the size of the circle.

How *fast* something spins is its **angular velocity**, written $\\omega$ (omega): how much angle it sweeps each second. Everyday spin rates are often given in **rpm** — revolutions (full turns) per minute. The time for one complete turn is the **period** $T$. So $60$ rpm means one turn per second, a period of $1$ second.

**Recap.** Rotation is motion around an axis; we measure the turn as an angle (degrees or radians), the spin rate as angular velocity (or rpm), and one turn takes a time called the period.

items.prompt: In rotational motion, what is the 'axis'?
choices: The fastest-moving point on the object
The fixed center line the object spins around
The outer edge of a spinning wheel
The total distance the object travels
items.explanation: The axis is the fixed line the object turns around, like the center pin of a merry-go-round. The outer edge actually moves fastest, but it is not the axis; the axis itself stays put.

items.prompt: One full turn all the way around a circle equals how many degrees?
items.explanation: A complete revolution is 360 degrees. 90 is a quarter turn and 180 is a half turn, so they are only parts of a full circle.

items.prompt: One full turn equals how many radians?
items.explanation: A radian is an angle measure where one whole circle is $2\\pi$ radians (about 6.28). $\\pi$ radians is only half a turn (180 degrees). 360 is the count in degrees, not radians.

items.prompt: What does 'angular velocity' (omega) measure?
choices: How much angle the object turns through each second
How far the object travels in a straight line
How heavy the spinning object is
items.explanation: Angular velocity is the spin rate: how much angle is swept per second. It is the rotational cousin of ordinary speed, but measured in angle instead of distance.

items.prompt: What does 'rpm' stand for?
choices: Rotations per minute (full turns each minute)
items.explanation: rpm means revolutions (complete turns) per minute, an everyday way to state how fast something spins. It counts whole turns, not radians or distance.

items.prompt: A wheel makes exactly one full turn every 2 seconds. What is its period?
choices: It has no period because it keeps spinning
items.explanation: The period T is the time for one complete turn, so it is 2 seconds here. 0.5 would be the number of turns per second (the rate), not the time per turn.

items.prompt: A fan spins at 60 rpm. How long does one full turn take?
items.explanation: 60 turns per minute is 60 turns per 60 seconds, which is exactly one turn per second, so the period is 1 second. Confusing 'per minute' with 'per turn' leads to the 60-second trap.

items.prompt: On a spinning merry-go-round, which rider sweeps through the SAME angle each second as a rider near the center?
choices: Only riders far from the center
Every rider, because the whole platform turns together
No rider, because they all turn at different angles
items.explanation: Because the platform is rigid, every point turns through the same angle in the same time, so all riders share one angular velocity. (Riders farther out cover more actual distance, but the same angle.)

items.prompt: Which of these is an example of rotational motion?
choices: A car driving straight down a highway
A book sliding across a flat table
A ceiling fan blade going around its center
A ball dropped straight down to the floor
items.explanation: Rotation means turning around an axis, which the fan blade does. The car, book, and falling ball all move in straight lines (translation), not around an axis.

items.prompt: A half turn (180 degrees) is the same as which angle in radians?
items.explanation: A full turn is $2\\pi$ radians, so half of that is $\\pi$ radians. $\\pi/2$ would be a quarter turn, and 180 is the degree count, not radians.

lessons.title: Torque: Force Times Lever Arm
lessons.content:
**Hook.** Try opening a heavy door by pushing right next to the hinges — it barely budges. Push at the far edge, by the handle, and it swings open easily. Same door, same hand, totally different result. The thing that changed is **torque** — the turning power of a push.

**Build.** A plain force is a push or pull. **Torque** is how much that push *twists* something around an axis. It depends on two things: how hard you push (the force $F$) and how far from the axis you push (the **lever arm** $r$, the distance from the axis to where the force acts). The rule is simply:

$$\\tau = F \\times r$$

read as "torque equals force times lever arm" ($\\tau$ is the Greek letter tau). That is why a long wrench loosens a stubborn bolt more easily than a short one: same hand force, bigger $r$, so more torque. It is also why door handles sit far from the hinges.

**Direction matters too.** A push only turns something well if it pushes *across* the lever arm (at a right angle). Pushing straight toward or away from the axis just squeezes or pulls — no turning. And pushing one way turns it clockwise; the opposite way turns it counterclockwise. On a balanced seesaw, the torque on each side is equal and opposite, so nothing turns.

**Recap.** Torque measures twisting and equals force times lever arm: push harder, or push farther from the axis, for more turn — and push in the right direction.

items.prompt: Torque is best described as:
choices: The total weight of an object
The twisting effect of a force around an axis
The straight-line speed of an object
items.explanation: Torque is the turning or twisting effect a force produces around an axis. Spin rate (angular velocity) and weight are different quantities entirely.

items.prompt: Torque equals force times what?
choices: The lever arm (distance from the axis to where the force acts)
items.explanation: Torque = force x lever arm. The lever arm r is how far from the axis you apply the force. Mass and speed do not appear in this rule.

items.prompt: Why is it easier to loosen a tight bolt with a LONG wrench than a short one?
choices: The long wrench is heavier, adding force
The long wrench applies the force farther from the axis, giving more torque
The long wrench changes the bolt's mass
Length has no effect; only your hand strength matters
items.explanation: A longer wrench means a bigger lever arm r, so the same hand force makes more torque. The wrench's own weight is not the point; the extra distance from the bolt is.

items.prompt: Where should a door handle be placed to make the door easiest to open?
choices: Halfway between hinges and far edge
At the far edge, as far from the hinges as possible
It makes no difference where the handle is
items.explanation: The hinges are the axis, so placing the handle far from them gives the largest lever arm and the most torque for your push. That is exactly where real door handles are.

items.prompt: You double how far from the axis you push, keeping the same force. The torque becomes:
items.explanation: Since torque = force x lever arm, doubling the lever arm doubles the torque. Force never changed, so the answer is exactly twice, not four times.

items.prompt: You push a wrench straight TOWARD the bolt (along the handle, aimed at the axis). How much does it turn?
choices: It barely turns; that push gives almost no torque
It turns the opposite way from normal
It turns exactly as much as a sideways push
items.explanation: A push aimed straight at the axis just squeezes inward and produces almost no turning. Torque is largest when you push across the lever arm, at a right angle to it.

items.prompt: On a seesaw, a child sits 2 meters from the pivot. To balance an equal child sitting 2 meters on the other side, the torques must be:
choices: Equal in size but opposite in turning direction
items.explanation: Balance means the clockwise torque on one side equals the counterclockwise torque on the other, so they cancel and nothing turns. Direction matters: they must oppose each other.

items.prompt: A heavy adult sits close to a seesaw's pivot while a light child sits far out. They can balance because:
choices: Torque depends only on weight, so the adult always wins
The child's larger lever arm can make up for the smaller force
The seesaw ignores where people sit
Lighter people always produce more torque
items.explanation: Torque = force x lever arm, so a small force far out can match a big force close in. This is why a child far from the pivot can balance a heavier adult sitting near it.

items.prompt: Two people push a merry-go-round at its edge with equal force but in OPPOSITE directions (one clockwise, one counterclockwise). What happens?
choices: Their torques cancel, so it does not start spinning
items.explanation: Equal torques in opposite directions cancel, leaving zero net torque, so the merry-go-round does not start turning. Direction of the push is just as important as its size.

items.prompt: Which change would NOT increase the torque you apply to a bolt?
choices: Pushing at a right angle to the wrench instead of along it
Pushing closer to the bolt with the same force
items.explanation: Pushing closer to the axis shrinks the lever arm, which lowers torque. Pushing harder, using a longer wrench, or pushing at a right angle all raise torque.

lessons.title: Moment of Inertia: Why Some Things Are Hard to Spin
lessons.content:
**Hook.** Hold a hammer by its head and wave it side to side — easy. Now hold it by the very end of the handle and wave it the same way — much harder, even though it's the exact same hammer. Nothing about its weight changed. What changed is how that weight is *spread out* relative to where you spin it. Physicists call that resistance to being spun the **moment of inertia**.

**Build.** You already know **inertia**: heavy things resist changes to their straight-line motion, which is why a loaded cart is hard to get rolling. The **moment of inertia** (symbol $I$) is the spinning version: it measures how much an object resists changes to its rotation. Here's the key surprise — $I$ depends not just on how much mass there is, but on *how far that mass sits from the axis*. Mass placed far out resists spinning far more than the same mass placed near the axis. In fact distance counts double: moving mass twice as far from the axis raises its moment of inertia by about four times.

This makes $I$ the rotational match to plain mass. Recall **Newton's second law**, $F = ma$ (force equals mass times acceleration) for straight-line motion. The rotational version is the same idea with new names: torque takes the place of force, and moment of inertia $I$ takes the place of mass. So for a given torque, a large $I$ means the object speeds up its spin only slowly.

**Recap.** Moment of inertia is rotational laziness: it grows when mass sits farther from the axis, and it is the spinning stand-in for mass in the rotational version of $F = ma$.

items.prompt: Moment of inertia measures:
choices: How fast an object is already spinning
How much an object resists changes to its rotation
The total torque applied to an object
The distance an object travels in a straight line
items.explanation: Moment of inertia is rotational stubbornness: how hard it is to start, stop, or change a spin. It is not the spin rate itself, and not the torque.

items.prompt: Two objects have the SAME total mass. Object A keeps its mass near the axis; object B has its mass spread far out. Which is harder to spin?
choices: Object B (mass far from the axis)
They are equally hard, since the mass is equal
items.explanation: Moment of inertia depends on how far the mass sits from the axis, not just on the total mass. Mass far out resists spinning more, so B is harder to spin even with equal mass.

items.prompt: Moment of inertia plays the same role in rotation that WHICH quantity plays in straight-line motion?
items.explanation: In the rotational version of Newton's second law, moment of inertia stands in for mass. Just as mass resists changes in straight-line motion, I resists changes in spin.

items.prompt: Newton's second law for straight-line motion is $F = ma$. The rotational version replaces force with torque and replaces mass with:
items.explanation: The rotational law has the same shape as $F = ma$, but torque takes the role of force and moment of inertia takes the role of mass. The lever arm is part of torque, not the mass stand-in.

items.prompt: Why is a hammer harder to wave when you hold it by the end of the handle (so the heavy head is far away)?
choices: The hammer gets heavier when held that way
The heavy head is now far from your hand (the axis), raising its moment of inertia
Gravity is stronger at the head
Holding it that way changes its mass
items.explanation: Same hammer, same mass, but the head is now far from the axis you spin it about, so its moment of inertia is large. Distance from the axis, not a weight change, makes it hard to wave.

items.prompt: You move a small weight from close to the axis to twice as far from the axis. Its contribution to the moment of inertia changes by roughly:
items.explanation: Distance from the axis counts double in moment of inertia, so doubling the distance multiplies the contribution by about four. This is why far-out mass matters so much.

items.prompt: For a fixed amount of torque, an object with a LARGE moment of inertia will:
choices: Speed up its spin very quickly
Speed up its spin only slowly
Refuse to move at all, forever
items.explanation: Just like a heavy object accelerates slowly under a given force, a large-I object gains spin slowly under a given torque. It still moves, just gradually.

items.prompt: A tightrope walker carries a long pole with weights on the ends. How does this help with balance?
choices: The far-out weights give a large moment of inertia, so the walker tips over only slowly
It removes gravity from the walker
It has no real effect; it is just for show
items.explanation: Weights far from the body create a large moment of inertia about the rope, so any tipping (rotation) builds up slowly, giving time to correct. The pole does not change the walker's weight.

items.prompt: Which factor does NOT directly affect an object's moment of inertia?
choices: How far the mass sits from the axis
What color the object is painted
items.explanation: Moment of inertia depends on the amount of mass, how far it is from the axis, and which axis you choose. Color has nothing to do with rotation.

items.prompt: Two wheels have equal mass. Wheel X has most of its mass in a heavy rim at the edge; wheel Y has most of its mass packed near the hub. Which has the larger moment of inertia?
choices: Equal, because the masses are equal
Neither, because wheels have no moment of inertia
items.explanation: Mass out at the rim is far from the axis, so wheel X has the larger moment of inertia despite equal total mass. Equal mass does not mean equal I when the mass sits at different distances.

lessons.title: Angular Momentum and Its Conservation
lessons.content:
**Hook.** Watch a figure skater spin. She starts slowly with her arms stretched wide, then pulls her arms in tight to her body — and suddenly she whirls much faster, without anyone pushing her. Where did the extra speed come from? The answer is one of the most beautiful rules in physics: the **conservation of angular momentum**.

**Build.** **Angular momentum** (symbol $L$) is how much spinning motion an object has. It combines two things you already know: its moment of inertia $I$ (how its mass is spread from the axis) and its angular velocity $\\omega$ (how fast it spins). In words, $L = I \\times \\omega$ — spin amount equals rotational laziness times spin rate.

The big rule: if nothing twists an object from outside (no outside torque), its angular momentum $L$ stays exactly the same. We say $L$ is **conserved**. Now the skater makes sense. Pulling her arms in moves her mass closer to the axis, which *lowers* her moment of inertia $I$. But $L$ must stay constant, so if $I$ goes down, the spin rate $\\omega$ must go *up* to compensate. Arms in, $I$ drops, she spins faster. Arms out, $I$ rises, she slows. She never pushed herself faster; she just rearranged her mass.

This same stubbornness of spin explains **gyroscopes** and **bicycles**: a spinning wheel resists changing the direction of its axis, which is what helps keep a moving bike upright.

**Recap.** Angular momentum $L = I \\times \\omega$ is conserved when no outside torque acts; pull mass inward and $I$ drops, so the spin must speed up — exactly why a skater spins faster with arms tucked in.

items.prompt: Angular momentum (L) combines which two quantities?
choices: Moment of inertia and angular velocity (spin rate)
items.explanation: Angular momentum is L = I x omega: moment of inertia times spin rate. Force and distance combine to make torque, which is a different idea.

items.prompt: Angular momentum stays constant ('is conserved') when:
choices: No outside torque twists the object
The object is painted a dark color
items.explanation: With no outside torque acting, angular momentum is conserved. Weight, color, and being at rest are not the condition; the absence of outside twisting is.

items.prompt: A spinning skater pulls her arms in and speeds up. Why does she speed up?
choices: Pulling her arms in pushes against the air to gain speed
Pulling in lowers her moment of inertia, so with angular momentum conserved her spin rate rises
She secretly pushes off the ice
items.explanation: Arms in moves mass toward the axis, lowering I. Since L = I x omega stays constant, a smaller I forces a larger omega, so she spins faster. No outside push is needed.

items.prompt: When the skater pulls her arms in, what happens to her moment of inertia?
choices: It decreases, because her mass is now closer to the axis
items.explanation: Moving mass closer to the axis lowers the moment of inertia. That drop in I is exactly what lets the spin rate rise while angular momentum stays constant.

items.prompt: A spinning skater stretches her arms back OUT. What happens to her spin rate?
items.explanation: Arms out raises I as mass moves away from the axis. With L conserved, a larger I means a smaller omega, so she slows down. This is just the reverse of tucking in.

items.prompt: As the skater pulls her arms in and speeds up, her angular momentum L:
items.explanation: With no outside torque, L is conserved, so it stays essentially constant. The trade is internal: I goes down and omega goes up, but their product L does not change.

items.prompt: A common wrong idea is that the skater 'creates' extra spinning motion by pulling in. What is the correct view?
choices: She does create new angular momentum from nothing
She keeps the same angular momentum but redistributes her mass, trading lower I for higher spin rate
She loses angular momentum but gains speed
Her speed is unrelated to her arms
items.explanation: No new angular momentum appears; L is conserved. She only rearranges her mass, lowering I, which forces omega up to keep L constant. Speed clearly does depend on her arms.

items.prompt: Why does a fast-spinning gyroscope or bicycle wheel resist tipping over?
choices: A spinning object resists changes to the direction of its spin axis (its angular momentum)
It has no moment of inertia while spinning
items.explanation: Angular momentum has a direction, and a spinning wheel resists changes to that direction, so it stays pointed where it is. This stability helps keep a moving bike upright.

items.prompt: A student on a rotating stool holds heavy weights out wide while slowly spinning, then pulls them to his chest. He will:
items.explanation: Pulling the weights inward lowers his moment of inertia, and with L conserved his spin rate rises, so he speeds up. This is the classic classroom version of the skater effect.

items.prompt: If a skater's moment of inertia drops to half its value (with no outside torque), her spin rate must:
items.explanation: Because L = I x omega stays constant, halving I forces omega to double so the product is unchanged. Thinking the spin rate halves too gets the relationship backwards.
